questions.stem: A 58-year-old man, a heavy smoker with a 40 pack-year history, presents with hoarseness that has persisted for 6 weeks. He has no odynophagia, cough, or weight loss. Flexible nasendoscopy reveals a lesion on the left vocal cord. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx
questions.explanation: Persistent hoarseness in a smoker over 40 is a red flag for laryngeal cancer. The lesion on nasendoscopy confirms a mass, making vocal cord polyp plausible but cancer is more likely given the risk factors. Laryngitis and reflux usually improve or have other features. Vocal cord palsy causes immobility, not a visible lesion.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman, a heavy drinker and smoker, presents with a painless, non-healing ulcer on the lateral border of her tongue that has been present for 3 months. She has no other symptoms. On examination, the ulcer has rolled edges and is indurated. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity
questions.explanation: A non-healing, indurated ulcer with rolled edges on the lateral tongue in a patient with risk factors (smoking, alcohol) is classic for oral squamous cell carcinoma. Aphthous ulcers are painful and heal within 2 weeks. Lichen planus is usually white reticulate patches. Leukoplakia is a white patch, not an ulcer. Papilloma is exophytic, not ulcerated.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of a painless lump in the front of her neck that moves when she swallows. She has no symptoms of hyper- or hypothyroidism. Ultrasound shows a solitary nodule in the left thyroid lobe with microcalcifications. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A solitary thyroid nodule with microcalcifications on ultrasound is highly suspicious for papillary thyroid carcinoma, the most common thyroid cancer. Adenoma can appear but microcalcifications are a malignant feature. Multinodular goitre has multiple nodules. Thyroglossal cyst is midline and elevates with tongue protrusion. Lymph node is outside the thyroid.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old male of Chinese descent presents with a 3-month history of left-sided nasal obstruction, occasional epistaxis, and a sensation of fullness in the left ear. Otoscopy shows a left middle ear effusion. He has a palpable left upper cervical lymph node. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of unilateral nasal symptoms, serous otitis media (from Eustachian tube obstruction), and cervical lymphadenopathy in a young Asian patient is classic for nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Rhinosinusitis usually involves purulent discharge and no effusion. Isolated OME is common in children. Lymphoma may present with nodal disease but not typically with nasal and ear symptoms. Tonsillitis causes sore throat, not unilateral obstruction.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with persistent right ear pain for 8 weeks. Otoscopy is normal. He has no hearing loss or discharge. He is a heavy smoker and drinks 40 units of alcohol per week. On examination, there is fullness in the right tonsillar fossa. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Referred otalgia from the oropharynx is a classic atypical presentation of oropharyngeal cancer. The tonsillar fossa fullness and risk factors point to malignancy. TMJ disorder, dental pain, and otitis media are common causes of ear pain but would have corresponding findings (e.g., tenderness, dental infection, abnormal otoscopy). Cervical spondylosis causes neck pain with radiation.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old smoker presents with a 4-week history of a firm, 2 cm left-sided neck lump. He has no other symptoms. What is the most appropriate first investigation to establish the diagnosis?
options: CT scan of neck and chest
questions.explanation: Ultrasound-guided FNAC is the first-line investigation for a suspicious neck lump, as it is minimally invasive, can differentiate benign from malignant, and guides further management. CT and MRI are useful for staging, not initial diagnosis; open biopsy risks seeding and should be avoided if possible; PET-CT is reserved for staging after confirmation.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old heavy smoker has had hoarseness for 6 weeks. There is no pain or dysphagia. Flexible nasendoscopy in clinic reveals an irregular lesion on the right vocal cord. What is the next best step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Direct laryngoscopy and biopsy under general anaesthesia
questions.explanation: Direct laryngoscopy and biopsy under GA allows full examination of the larynx and histopathological confirmation of the suspected cancer. Imaging (CT) is for staging once diagnosis is confirmed; repeat nasendoscopy or stroboscopy would delay definitive diagnosis; sputum cytology is not diagnostic for laryngeal lesions.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with a non-healing ulcer on the lateral border of her tongue, present for 3 weeks. She drinks 30 units of alcohol per week and smoked 20 cigarettes/day until 5 years ago. There is no palpable neck lymphadenopathy. What is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: MRI of the head and neck
questions.explanation: Incisional biopsy (taking a wedge of the lesion including part of normal tissue) is the gold standard for confirming oral cancer, providing adequate tissue for histology and margin assessment. Exfoliative cytology may miss invasive disease; imaging is for staging after histological confirmation.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 3 cm, firm, solitary thyroid nodule discovered incidentally on self-palpation. She is euthyroid and asymptomatic. What is the best initial test to establish the nature of the nodule?
questions.explanation: Fine needle aspiration (FNA) cytology is the test of choice for evaluating a thyroid nodule >1 cm, as it provides cytological diagnosis (Bethesda system). TFTs assess function only; ultrasound assesses features but does not confirm malignancy; radioiodine scan is for functional nodules; CT is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man is found to have a metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in a cervical lymph node after excision biopsy. There is no obvious primary lesion on thorough clinical examination, flexible nasendoscopy, and panendoscopy. What is the most appropriate imaging investigation to locate the occult primary?
options: CT scan of the chest, abdomen and pelvis
MRI of the head and neck
questions.explanation: PET-CT is the most sensitive imaging modality for detecting an occult primary in the head and neck region and also screens for distant metastases. CT and MRI have lower sensitivity; ultrasound is for nodal assessment; bone scan is not indicated for primary detection.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a non-healing ulcer on the lateral border of his tongue for 6 weeks. Biopsy confirms a well-differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. CT neck shows a 1.8 cm tumour confined to the tongue, no nodal metastases. He is otherwise fit and well. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
questions.explanation: Surgery with elective neck dissection is the standard of care for early-stage oral cancer (T1-2N0), offering high cure rates and avoiding late effects of radiotherapy. Radiotherapy alone is an alternative if surgery is contraindicated, but the patient is fit for surgery.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history presents with hoarseness and stridor. Laryngoscopy shows a transglottic tumour involving the left true vocal cord and supraglottis with fixation of the cord. CT shows a 3 cm tumour with ipsilateral level II node (2 cm). Staging is T3N1M0. He is keen to preserve his voice. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For locally advanced laryngeal cancer (T3N1) in a patient wishing for organ preservation, concurrent chemoradiotherapy (with cisplatin) is the recommended first-line treatment, achieving similar survival to total laryngectomy while preserving the larynx.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man previously treated with radical radiotherapy for a T2N0 squamous cell carcinoma of the tonsil 18 months ago presents with pain in the right jaw and exposed bone intraorally for 4 weeks. Examination reveals a 1 cm area of exposed, necrotic mandibular bone. He is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
questions.explanation: Osteoradionecrosis of the mandible is a known complication of radiotherapy. Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is first-line as it promotes angiogenesis and tissue healing. Antibiotics and sequestrectomy are adjunctive or for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with newly diagnosed recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (oral cavity primary) has PD-L1 expression with a combined positive score (CPS) of 25. He has not received prior systemic therapy for metastatic disease. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends pembrolizumab monotherapy as first-line for recurrent/metastatic HNSCC in patients with PD-L1 CPS ≥20. Nivolumab is reserved for platinum-refractory disease.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and ischaemic heart disease presents with hoarseness. Laryngoscopy shows a T1a squamous cell carcinoma of the right vocal cord limited to the middle third. He is not fit for general anaesthesia due to his comorbidities. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
questions.explanation: For early glottic cancer (T1a) in a patient unfit for general anaesthesia, radiotherapy is the preferred non-invasive option. Transoral laser surgery requires anaesthesia.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male smoker presents to the emergency department with a three-week history of progressive hoarseness and a palpable right-sided neck lump. He has no stridor or dysphagia. Observations are normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to ENT under 2-week wait pathway
Check full blood count and ESR
Request flexible laryngoscopy in the emergency department
Arrange urgent CT neck with contrast
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend urgent referral (2-week wait) for any patient with persistent hoarseness >3 weeks and a neck lump, as this combination is highly suspicious for laryngeal cancer. Imaging or endoscopy should be arranged by the specialist, not initiated in ED.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with T1a glottic squamous cell carcinoma treated with radiotherapy 18 months ago now reports worsening hoarseness over the past month. He has no stridor, dysphagia, or palpable neck nodes. Flexible laryngoscopy in clinic shows an irregular lesion at the original tumour site. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent CT neck and chest
Reassure and arrange review in 3 months
Repeat laryngoscopy and biopsy under general anaesthetic
Refer to speech and language therapy for vocal rehabilitation
questions.explanation: Persistent or worsening hoarseness after radiotherapy raises concern for residual or recurrent disease. The gold standard for diagnosis is direct laryngoscopy and biopsy. Imaging (CT) may be adjunctive but does not replace tissue diagnosis, and reassurance is inappropriate given the concerning laryngoscopy findings.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with known advanced supraglottic laryngeal cancer presents with acute onset of inspiratory stridor, drooling, and oxygen saturation 88% on room air. She is sitting upright and distressed. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In a stridor patient with signs of impending airway compromise, the priority is to call for experienced anaesthetic help (senior anaesthetist/ENT surgeon) for definitive airway management. While steroids may reduce oedema, they are not the immediate life-saving step; cricothyroidotomy is reserved for 'can't intubate, can't ventilate' scenarios.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man underwent chemoradiotherapy for T3 nasopharyngeal carcinoma 6 months ago. He now reports progressive difficulty opening his mouth. On examination, mouth opening is limited to 2 cm with no mucosal ulceration or trismus pain. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Prescribe botulinum toxin injection to the masseter muscles
Refer to physiotherapy for jaw-stretching exercises
Start a course of oral prednisolone
Arrange urgent MRI of the nasopharynx
Refer to maxillofacial surgery for consideration of coronoidectomy
questions.explanation: Post-radiotherapy trismus is often due to fibrosis of the masticatory muscles. First-line management in the UK is physiotherapy with jaw-stretching exercises. While MRI can exclude recurrence, the absence of other symptoms favours benign fibrosis. Steroids and botulinum toxin are not first-line, and surgery is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity (not amenable to curative treatment) is admitted from home with poor oral intake over the past week, weight loss, and mild dehydration. She has no dysphagia or airway compromise. Her family asks about feeding options. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Insert a peripheral cannula and start intravenous fluids and parenteral nutrition
Refer to dietitian for oral nutritional supplements
Discuss goals of care and consider best supportive care with patient and family
questions.explanation: In an elderly patient with advanced incurable cancer, the immediate priority is to discuss goals of care. Artificial nutrition may not improve quality of life or survival and can lead to complications. A shared decision with the patient and family should precede any intervention. IV fluids for hydration may be appropriate, but not TPN.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with T3N1M0 squamous cell carcinoma of the larynx, currently awaiting radiotherapy, presents to the emergency department with a two-day history of progressive dyspnoea. On examination, he is sitting upright, tachypnoeic, and has inspiratory stridor at rest. Oxygen saturations are 94% on room air. Which single clinical feature most clearly indicates that this patient requires urgent airway intervention?
questions.explanation: Stridor at rest signifies critical airway narrowing and impending complete obstruction; it is the key red flag for immediate ENT referral for possible tracheostomy. Hoarseness and dysphagia are common in laryngeal cancer but do not indicate acute emergency.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of heavy smoking and alcohol use presents with a two-month history of a left-sided neck lump. He has recently developed pain radiating to his left ear and a 'husky' voice. On examination, there is a firm, non-tender, fixed left level II lymph node measuring 3 cm. Which single finding is a 'red flag' for advanced oropharyngeal cancer?
questions.explanation: Referred otalgia (pain felt in the ear from a pharyngeal source) indicates deep invasion of the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) or vagus nerve and is a classic red flag for oropharyngeal cancer, often signifying advanced disease. Hoarseness is also a symptom, but otalgia is more specific for invasion.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman who completed chemoradiotherapy for nasopharyngeal carcinoma six months ago presents with new-onset diplopia and a headache. On examination, she has an inability to abduct her right eye. Which finding should raise immediate concern for skull base recurrence?
questions.explanation: New-onset sixth nerve palsy (causing diplopia) in a patient with nasopharyngeal cancer suggests erosion into the skull base or cavernous sinus involvement, a red flag for aggressive recurrence requiring urgent imaging. Other symptoms are less specific.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a history of human papillomavirus (HPV)-positive tonsillar cancer, treated with primary radiotherapy 18 months ago, presents with acute onset of severe throat pain, drooling, and a 'hot potato' voice. On examination, there is marked swelling of the right tonsil and soft palate extending to the midline. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests an emergency requiring immediate ENT assessment?
questions.explanation: Stridor indicates airway narrowing at or above the larynx. In a patient with prior head and neck cancer, new stridor from oropharyngeal swelling signals impending airway obstruction, most likely due to recurrent tumour, and is the key red flag for urgent airway intervention. Trismus is also concerning but does not immediately threaten the airway.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of laryngeal cancer treated with total laryngectomy and adjuvant radiotherapy one year ago presents with acute bleeding from his stoma. The bleeding is bright red and pulsatile. Which clinical feature indicates a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Pulsatile bleeding from a stoma in a patient with prior laryngectomy and radiotherapy suggests a carotid artery blowout (carotid rupture), a catastrophic emergency. Any bleeding is concerning, but pulsatility and flow indicate arterial origin, requiring immediate pressure and vascular surgery involvement.
